## [00:00 - 03:02]

Guys, so today we're going to be looking at the creative and direct response in your ads. This is really, really important because it doesn't matter how good your targeting is. It doesn't matter how amazing your landing page is or your offer. If you're not communicating it properly, no one's going to get it, right? No one's going to resonate with it. No one's going to notice the ad in their news feed and no one's going to click on it. So this is an integral part of being a successful Facebook marketer. Certainly don't need to have HTTP. Just make it the brand and then dot com or dot code or whatever the the dot is, right, the the URL is. ## [09:08 - 12:13]

audience of people that have messaged your page, right? So, you can only use this ad with a custom audience of people that have already messaged your page. So, in essence, these are what we call a warm audience. They've already sent you a message, so they have interacted with you before. So, what you want to bear in mind with your creative or your copy or your content here is that you want to make your language a little more informal than what you might normally do because you are speaking to people that have already messaged your page. Now, at the moment, you can only create a custom audience of everyone that has messaged your page within a certain timeframe. So, some people might have messaged for complaints, some people might have messaged for product inquiries. So, this is probably another one of the most popular ads that we see out there. And the reason for this is that Facebook loves video. They have made no secret that they're basically trying to dominate the video space. They're trying to take YouTube down and the TV advertising spends. So, they are really preferring video. At the moment, you can get video views for as low as two cents, three cents a video view. It's cheap as chips. So, the format of the video is 16 by 9. Now, if you're going to go on mobile, I would recommend that you always use either a square or a vertical video because it's going to dominate a lot more real estate on that news feed. Whereas, if you go square or vertical, it's going to take up almost all of that news feed real estate while they're scrolling past. So, it really captures the eye. To do this, to upload a square or a vertical, you need to make sure that the video is already in those dimensions when you upload it. So, if you're going to upload a square video, you have to make sure it is exactly one by one by the time you upload it into Facebook. Now, for awareness type videos, you want to keep this nice and short and sweet, especially

## [18:19 - 21:21]

So are you retargeting people that abandon their shopping cart? If that's the case, you kind of want to remind them of that in a copy, right? You want to remind them that, hey, you left some stuff in your cart or hey, you know, don't forget these deals, they're going to expire soon. So normally what Facebook recommends is running with some bright, bold, or high contrast images. So what you want to remember is that people are on Facebook to check out photos of their friends, to check out photos of their family or their kids. So you really want to sort of have creative that is going to disrupt that newsfeed. You're competing against photos that are very high affinity, right? So it's entertaining content and then it's their contacts, their friends, their family, their peers. So what you want to do is stand out and what they recommend is bright, bold, colors, or high contrast. We've also found that images with smiling women, especially when you're targeting a male audience, works really, really well for a high click through rates. And actually kittens, sadly, it is true. Your kittens and puppies do get higher click through rates as well. One of the other tricks that is not used a lot, however, for higher click through rates is using emojis in the copy. So obviously only do this if it's brand appropriate. But another thing that you can look at as well is alt codes. So where you're trying to get someone to take an action right away, such as buying or registering or filling out a form, you want to include a link in the copy as well. Now what we found is with e-commerce, normally an unshortened link works better. And with service professionals and coaches, a shortened link works better. So you're using a tool like Google URL shortener or Bitly. Now another thing to bear in mind is that the 20% rule no longer applies. So we used to have this rule where if you had more than 20% of text on your image, Facebook would not let you run your ad. Well, this is no longer in place. However, Facebook has said that the more text your image has, the more you will be penalized in the auction and the less reach you are likely to get.

## [21:21 - 24:22]

So essentially you're going to be paying a lot more for your ad. So if you can, you want to try to avoid using text if it's possible at all. If you do use text, don't try to fill up your entire image with it. Use as little as you can get away with. Now some of the big no-nos in terms of creative is, and these are reasons why your ads may have been pulled down or shut down, is that these are things that Facebook really doesn't like to see. So the first thing is references to mental or physical illnesses or disabilities such as autism or Asperger's or depression. You know what? Even back pain, we've seen ads have that problem. So that can get you flagged as well. So if you are working in a space where that is, you know, the solution is to one of those problems is to a mental or physical illness or disability. What you want to do is try to not use that word if at all possible and use different copy to get people to realize that's what you're talking about without actually saying it if that makes sense. So depression instead of using the word depression, you know, are you feeling down or things like that. Now naked men and women, obviously that's a big no-no. But what we found is also men just with their chest out can get red flags and can get the photo disapproved. Women and bikinis, it's on and off. What we found is you can actually mostly get away with it. But occasionally if it's too sexual in nature, Facebook is going to flag it as well. Trademarks for obvious reasons. So if you've got a client that is swiping things and has, you know, the walking dead or anything like that in their ads, that can definitely get you red flagged if not shut down the entire ad account. Now bear in mind the supplies for your videos as well and your songs. So any music that you're using in your videos, if those are trademark songs. So if you're using Beyonce's song in the background of your video, that is a big way to get yourself shut down by Facebook really quickly. Now mentions are claims associated with the word you. And you is a very powerful psychological hook. So you can still use the word you. In fact, a lot of our ads use the word you, but just understand that if it got disapproved or if it's not feeding out properly, that might be one of the problems.

## [24:22 - 27:24]

Facebook also doesn't like really big, bold claims that can never be achieved. So loose 20 pounds in under a month, for example, is obviously clickbaiting. It's not really humanly possible and Facebook will not like those types of claims. It can often have a problem as well around money claims. So if you're working with coaches and you would like to, you know, her webinar is titled Five Ways to Make $100,000 in Under 30 Days, that could get you red flagged as well. And weight loss and before and after photos, Facebook also has a problem with. Now when you have an ad that gets disapproved, if you feel like it doesn't cross over on any of these or it's not, you know, abusing any of their terms, sometimes Facebook gets it wrong. Sometimes Facebook will disapprove ads and actually you're not doing anything wrong with it. So what you have to do in that situation is they will allow you to refute it and you can actually just refute the disapproval and just keep doing that. Just keep doing it until it gets escalated and a real human being gets behind that thread, I guess, that objection thread and has a look at the ad, not the algorithm. So if you're in a situation where an ad was disapproved, it doesn't always necessarily mean you should just give up and create a brand new ad. If you 100% feel that you're following all the rules and it got disapproved, just refute it and normally within the next 24 to 48 hours, someone from Facebook will have had a look at it and if it's good, will tell you that, yeah, we've manually approved it. ## [30:27 - 33:30]

All right. So let's look at some of the copy for the different verticals that you might be working with. Now when you're doing e-commerce you want to use nice sharp sharp copy. This is definitely what we call direct response. You're trying to get people to buy the product. So this is not necessarily top of funnel where you're raising awareness of the product. This is you know buy my stuff. Here's an ad. It's going to take you to the sales page. I want you to buy this product. So in this situation you want to use nice short sharp copy. Do not try to get cute with this. Sometimes we see headlines saying oh the the treasury is now open. Don't try to get cute with that. It doesn't work with direct response. You want to make it short. You want to make it very clear what the product is. The offer is why they should click. Now in this example we're using 10% off free global shipping. Some really popular direct responses say 50% click to buy now. You might have a little bit of copy that says you know the official the official the official product that lets you do XNF say 50% click to buy now limited stock for example. That would be some very common direct response. And then in fact I would even encourage you to have the link there as well in the copy. But let's say it's not on brand. So let's say you're working with an e-commerce client and they don't want to discount and they don't want it to you know they're more of a luxury brand or high end e-commerce. Some of the direct response you then want to use is just remind them of the watch and maybe of the product and maybe just sell them limited stock selling out things like that. So you don't you can still use direct response without using you know save now or say 50% or click to buy now or all of that stuff. But you still want to keep it sharp and to the point and create some urgency in some shape or form. Now if we're looking at the service sector so in this example this realtor is essentially giving a free home valuation to capture leads. So this is obviously an example ad here but what we're looking at here is a cross in terms of the copy between e-commerce is very short so you're going to use nice short copy and we'll normally start up here. With service sector type brands it's not going to be long form like a coach consultant or online course would be but it's going to be sitting somewhere in the middle. So you're not going to get away if it's too short. It's not enough for people to fully understand and give you details but you will get away if it's somewhere in the middle between long form and short form. And the reason for this is that normally these types of clients are asking for leads and

normally a lead is an email or a phone number or a quote request right. ## [36:32 - 39:35]

If you went through the training module on how to build your own funnel or the verticals to you sorry you will have learned this the strategy that we use here. So essentially it's three slides that we have the first one is called the awesome the second is called the bad and the third one is called the without even if. And all we do when we go through these is we go what is the target audience what is the offer. Okay so what is the awesome scenario what is the scenario that they want to be what do they want in their life or their business that my client can help them with right what would be the perfect scenario is they worked with my with my client with my coaching client. What would be the bad so if they didn't work with my coaching client what could potentially happen you know where are they at right now what are the pain points. And then what do they think they have to do or what objections do they have what beliefs or misconceptions do they have that they think needs to happen in order to achieve the awesome but that really aren't true. So some of the tips that I have here to help you craft some really great direct response is the first thing you want to do is actually look at client testimonials of results that your client previously achieved and you can pull from them. So let's say your client helped one let's say your coaching client help one of his clients make $30,000 in one month. Well obviously that's a bullet you want to use here make $30,000 in four weeks or you know find your soulmate in under two months whatever results that you got that's some of the bullets that you want to start building from the actual testimonials. You can also go on Amazon.com and you can start searching books around the industry that your client works in.
